en.extract:
The Asian koel (Eudynamys scolopaceus) is a member of the cuckoo family of birds, the Cuculidae. It is found in the Indian subcontinent,  China, and Southeast Asia. It forms a superspecies with the closely related black-billed koels, and Pacific koels which are sometimes treated as subspecies. The Asian koel, like many of its related cuckoo kin, is a brood parasite that lays its eggs in the nests of crows and other hosts, who raise its young. They are unusual among the cuckoos in being largely frugivorous as adults. The name koel is echoic in origin with several language variants.  The bird is a widely used symbol in Indian, Pakistani and Nepali poetry.


Taxonomy
In 1747, the English naturalist George Edwards included an illustration and a description of an Asian koel in the second volume of his A Natural History of Uncommon Birds book. He used the English name "The Brown and Spotted Indian Cuckow". Edwards based his hand-coloured etching on a specimen from Bengal that belonged to the London silk-pattern designer and naturalist Joseph Dandridge. When in 1758 the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus updated his Systema Naturae for the tenth edition, he placed the Asian koel with the other cuckoos in the genus Cuculus. Linnaeus included a brief description, coined the binomial name Cuculus scolopaceus and cited Edwards' work. The Asian koel is now placed in the genus Eudynamys that was introduced in 1827 by the English naturalists Nicholas Vigors and Thomas Horsfield. The genus name Eudynamys combines the Ancient Greek eu meaning "fine" with dunamis meaning "power" or "strength". The specific epithet scolopaceus is Modern Latin meaning "snipe-like" from Latin scolopax meaning "snipe" or "woodcock".
The species has variations within its wide range with several island populations and a number of taxonomic variations have been suggested. The black-billed koel (E. melanorhynchus) of the Sulawesi region and the Pacific koel of Australasia are sometimes considered conspecific with the Asian koel in which case the "combined" species is known as the common koel. Due to differences in plumage, colour of bill and voice, the three are increasingly treated as separate species. Alternatively, only the black-billed koel has been considered as a separate species, or the Asian koel has included all subspecies otherwise included in the Pacific koel, except for the subspecies breeding in Australia, which then has the name Australian koel (E. cyanocephalus).
The Asian koel has several geographic forms that have well marked plumage differences or have been geographically isolated with little gene flow. The following is a list of named subspecies with their distributions and synonyms as given by Payne:

E. s. scolopaceus (Linnaeus, 1758) – Pakistan, India, Nepal, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Laccadives and Maldives
E. s. chinensis Cabanis and Heine, 1863 – Southern China and Indochina, except the Thai-Malay Peninsula
E. s. harterti Ingram, C 1912 – Hainan
E. s. malayana Cabanis and Heine, 1863 – Thai-Malay Peninsula, Lesser Sundas and Greater Sundas, except Sulawesi. May include the race dolosa described from the Andaman and Nicobar Islands
E. s. mindanensis (Linnaeus, 1766) – (includes E. s. paraguena) (Hachisuka, 1934) from Palawan, and E. s. corvina (Stresemann, 1931) from Halmahera, the Philippines (including Palawan and the Babuyan Islands), islands between Mindanao and Sulawesi, and North Maluku, except the Sula Islands


Description
 
The Asian koel is a large and long-tailed cuckoo measuring 39–46 cm (15–18 in) and weighing 190–327 g (6.7–11.5 oz). The male of the nominate race is glossy bluish-black, with a pale greenish grey bill, the iris is crimson, and it has grey legs and feet. The female of the nominate race is brownish on the crown and has rufous streaks on the head. The back, rump and wing coverts are dark brown with white and buff spots. The underparts are whitish, but is heavily striped. The other subspecies differ in colouration and size. The upper plumage of young birds is more like that of the male and they have a black beak.
They are very vocal during the breeding season (March to August in the Indian Subcontinent), with a range of different calls. The familiar song of the male is a repeated koo-Ooo. The female makes a shrill kik-kik-kik... call. Calls vary across populations.
They show a pattern of moult that differs from those of other parasitic cuckoos. The outer primaries show a transilient (alternating) ascending moult (P9-7-5-10-8-6) while the inner primaries are moulted in stepwise descending order (1-2-3-4).(Payne citing Stresemann and Stresemann 1961)


Distribution and habitat

The Asian koel is a bird of light woodland and cultivation. It is a mainly resident breeder in tropical southern Asia from Iran, Pakistan, India, Bangladesh, Maldives, and Sri Lanka to southern China and the Greater Sundas. They have great potential in colonizing new areas, and were among the pioneer birds to colonize the volcanic island of Krakatau. They first arrived in Singapore in the 1980s and became very common birds.
Some populations may make long-distance movements being found in places like Australia.


Behaviour

The Asian koel is a brood parasite, and lays its single egg in the nests of a variety of birds, including the jungle crow, and house crow. In Sri Lanka before 1880 it was only known to parasitize the jungle crow, later shifting to the house crow. A study in India found 5% of Corvus splendens and 0.5% of Corvus macrorhynchos nests parasitized.

In Bangladesh, they parasitise long-tailed shrike (Lanius schach), common myna (Acridotheres tristis) and house crows (Corvus splendens) at about 35.7, 31.2 and 10.8% rates respectively. Host nests at low heights and nearer to fruit trees tended to be preferred by koels. In Southern Thailand and the Malay Peninsula, koels have shifted host from crows to mynas (Acridotheres sp.) as the latter became more common in the late 1900s. A record exists of black drongo raising a koel, and other hosts recorded include the European magpie and possibly the black-headed oriole. In the Philippines, Asian Koels have been found to parasitize the cavity nest of Coletos (Sarcops calvus). Males may distract the hosts so that the female gets a chance to lay an egg in the nest. More often however, the female visits the nest of the host alone. The koel is not known to lay eggs in an empty host nest and a study in Pakistan found that the first koel eggs were laid, on average, within one and half days of the laying of the host's first egg. The chicks of the koel hatched about 3 days ahead of the host chicks. Koels usually lay only an egg or two in a single nest but as many as seven to eleven eggs have been reported from some host nests. A female may remove a host egg before laying. Eggs hatch in 12 to 14 days. The young koel does not always push out eggs or evict the host chicks, and initially calls like a crow. The young fledge in 20 to 28 days. Unlike some other cuckoos, the young do not attempt to kill the host chicks, a trait that is shared with the channel-billed cuckoos which are also largely frugivorous as adults. Some scholars, Rebecca Kilner in particular, have suggested that koels, like some other brood parasites, do not evict the host chicks as a result of higher costs which might outweigh the benefits of evicting nestmates. A small parasite may not be able to evict large host eggs or chicks from a deep Corvid nest without risking starvation and possibly accidental self-eviction. An alternative hypothesis that retaining host chicks might benefit the koel chicks did not gain much support. Adult female parents have been known to feed young koels in the nests of the hosts, a behaviour seen in some other brood parasitic species as well. Adult males have however not been noted to feed fledglings.
The Asian koel is omnivorous, consuming a variety of insects, caterpillars, eggs and small vertebrates. Adults feed mainly on fruit. They will sometimes defend fruiting trees that they forage in and chase away other frugivores. They have been noted to be especially important in the dispersal of the sandalwood tree (Santalum album) in India. Large seeded fruits are sometimes quickly regurgitated near the parent tree while small seeded fruits are ingested and are likely to be deposited at greater distances from the parent tree. They have a large gape and are capable of swallowing large fruits including the hard fruit of palms such as Arenga and Livistona. They have been known to occasionally take eggs of small birds.
They feed on the fruits of Cascabela thevetia which are known to be toxic to mammals.
A number of parasites of the species have been described, including malaria-like protozoa, lice and nematodes.


In culture
The word "koel" is onomatopoeic in origin. The Sanskrit name of "Kokila" and words in several Indian languages are similarly echoic. Being familiar birds with loud calls, references to them are common in folklore, myth and poetry. It is traditionally held in high regard for its song and revered in the Manusmriti, with a decree protecting them from harm. The Vedas, Sanskrit literature dated to about 2000 BC referred to it as Anya-Vapa which has been translated as "that which was raised by others" (or "sown for others to reap"). This has been interpreted as the earliest written reference to brood parasitism.  It has been chosen as the state bird by the Indian union territory of Puducherry.
These birds were once very popular in India as cagebirds. Feeding even on boiled rice, these hardy birds lived in captivity for as long as 14 years.
In Sri Lanka, Koel has an unbreakable bond with the Sinhalese New Year as according to the beliefs, the call of koels signify the arrival of the new year in the month of April.


References


Further reading
Eates, KR (1938) The status of the Koel (Eudynamys scolopaceus L.) in Sind. Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society 40(2):328.
Jose, TV (1980) Koels. Newsletter for Birdwatchers . 20(4), 17.
Menon, G.K.; Shah, R.V. (1979) Adaptive features in juvenal plumage pattern of the Indian Koel Eudynamys scolopacea: host mimesis and hawk-pattern. Journal of the Yamashina Institute for Ornithology 11(2):87–95
Neelakantan, KK (1980) The breeding of the Indian Koel Eudynamys scolopacea. Newsletter for Birdwatchers . 20(1):7.
Parasharya, BM (1985) Comments on eye color in the Koel Eudynamys scolopacea. Newsletter for Birdwatchers . 25(1–2), 11–12.
Ray-Chaudhuri, R. (1967) Mitotic and meiotic chromosomes of the koel Eudynamys scolopacea scolopacea. Nucleus 10: 179–189. (Study notes that the W chromosome is the 6th largest one and has the centromere in a subterminal position.)
Ryall C (2003) Mimicry of a crow chick by an Asian koel Eudynamys scolopacea as a defence against attack by house crows Corvus splendens. Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society 100(1):136–137
Santharam, V (1979) Crows feeding young koel. Newsletter for Birdwatchers . 19(7), 4.
Sarkar AK, Maitra SK, Midya T. (1976) Histological, histochemical and biochemical effects of cadmium chloride in female koel (Eudynamys scolopacea). Acta Histochem. 57(2):205-11.
Sarkar AK, Maitra SK, Midya T. (1977) Effects of reserpine on female reproductive organs of the Indian koel Eudynamys scolopacea scolopacea (L). Indian Journal of Experimental Biology 15(5):349-51.

The bird is a widely used symbol in Indian, Pakistani and Nepali poetry.{{Cite web |last=Farooqi |first=Mehr Afshan |date=2022-04-10 |title=COLUMN: THE BIRDS OF URDU POETRY |url=https://www.dawn.com/news/1684326 |access-date=2025-11-23 |website=Dawn |language=en}}{{Additional citation needed|date=November 2020}} == Taxonomy == In 1747, the English naturalist [[George Edwards (naturalist)|George Edwards]] included an illustration and a description of an Asian koel in the second volume of his ''A Natural History of Uncommon Birds'' book. He used the English name "The Brown and Spotted Indian Cuckow". Edwards based his hand-coloured etching on a specimen from Bengal that belonged to the London silk-pattern designer and naturalist [[Joseph Dandridge]].{{ cite book | last=Edwards | first=George | author-link=George Edwards (naturalist) | year=1747 | title=A Natural History of Uncommon Birds | location=London | publisher=Printed for the author at the College of Physicians | volume=Part II | page=59, Plate 59 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/50240729 }} When in 1758 the Swedish naturalist [[Carl Linnaeus]] updated his ''[[Systema Naturae]]'' for the [[10th edition of Systema Naturae|tenth edition]], he placed the Asian koel with the other cuckoos in the [[genus]] ''[[Cuculus]]''. Linnaeus included a brief description, coined the [[binomial name]] ''Cuculus scolopaceus'' and cited Edwards' work.{{cite book | last=Linnaeus | first=Carl | author-link=Carl Linnaeus | year=1758 | title= Systema Naturae per regna tria naturae, secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis | volume=1 | edition=10th | page=111 | publisher=Laurentii Salvii | location=Holmiae (Stockholm) | language=Latin | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/727016 }} The Asian koel is now placed in the [[genus]] ''[[Eudynamys]]'' that was introduced in 1827 by the English naturalists [[Nicholas Aylward Vigors|Nicholas Vigors]] and [[Thomas Horsfield]].{{ cite journal | last1=Vigors | first1=Nicholas Aylward | author1-link=Nicholas Aylward Vigors | last2=Horsfield | first2=Thomas | author2-link=Thomas Horsfield | year=1827 | title=Australian birds in the collection of the Linnean Society; with an attempt at arranging them according to their natural affinities | journal=Transactions of the Linnean Society of London | volume=15 | issue=1 | language=English, Latin| pages=170–334 [303] | doi=10.1111/j.1095-8339.1826.tb00115.x | url=https://zenodo.org/record/1447478 }} The title page is dated 1826 but the article was not published until 1827.{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=July 2021 | title=Turacos, bustards, cuckoos, mesites, sandgrouse | work=IOC World Bird List Version 11.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/turacos/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=7 October 2021}} The genus name ''Eudynamys'' combines the [[Ancient Greek]] ''eu'' meaning "fine" with ''dunamis'' meaning "power" or "strength". The specific epithet ''scolopaceus'' is [[Neo-Latin|Modern Latin]] meaning "snipe-like" from Latin ''scolopax'' meaning "snipe" or "woodcock".{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | pages=[https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n152/mode/1up 152], [https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n351/mode/1up 351] }} The species has variations within its wide range with several island populations and a number of taxonomic variations have been suggested. The [[black-billed koel]] (''E. melanorhynchus'') of the [[Sulawesi]] region and the [[Pacific koel]] of Australasia are sometimes considered [[conspecific]] with the Asian koel in which case the "combined" species is known as the [[common koel]]. Due to differences in [[plumage]], colour of bill and voice, the three are increasingly treated as separate species.{{cite web|author=Gill, F.|author2=Wright, M.|author3=Donsker, D.|name-list-style=amp|year=2009|url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/|title=IOC World Bird Names version 2.0|access-date=2009-04-21}}{{cite book|author1=Christidis, L |author2=Boles, W E |name-list-style=amp|year=2008|title=Systematics and Taxonomy of Australian Birds|isbn=978-0-643-06511-6|publisher=CSIRO Publishing|location=Collingwood, Vic.}} Alternatively, only the black-billed koel has been considered as a separate species, or the Asian koel has included all subspecies otherwise included in the Pacific koel, except for the subspecies breeding in Australia, which then has the name [[Australian koel]] (''E. cyanocephalus'').{{cite book|author=Clements, JF|year=2007|title=The Clements Checklist of the Birds of the World.|edition=7th|publisher=Christopher Helm|isbn=978-0-7136-8695-1}}{{cite journal|author=Penart TE|year=1919|title=The name of the Black Cuckoo|pages=569–570| url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v036n04/p0569-p0570.pdf| journal=Auk| volume=36| issue=4|doi=10.2307/4073368|jstor=4073368 }} The Asian koel has several geographic forms that have well marked plumage differences or have been geographically isolated with little gene flow. The following is a list of named subspecies with their distributions and [[Synonym (taxonomy)|synonyms]] as given by Payne: *''E. s. scolopaceus'' ([[Carl Linnaeus|Linnaeus]], [[10th edition of Systema Naturae|1758]]) – Pakistan, India, [[Nepal]], Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, [[Laccadives]] and [[Maldives]] *''E. s. chinensis'' [[Jean Cabanis|Cabanis]] and [[Ferdinand Heine|Heine]], 1863 – Southern China and [[Indochina]], except the [[Thai-Malay Peninsula]] *''E. s. harterti'' [[Collingwood Ingram|Ingram, C]] 1912 – [[Hainan]] *''E. s. malayana'' Cabanis and Heine, 1863 – [[Thai-Malay Peninsula]], [[Lesser Sundas]] and [[Greater Sundas]], except [[Sulawesi]]. May include the race ''dolosa'' described from the [[Andaman Islands|Andaman]] and [[Nicobar Islands]]{{cite journal|author=Ripley, SD|author-link=Sidney Dillon Ripley|year=1946|title=The Koels of the Bay of Bengal.|journal=The Auk|volume=63|issue=2|pages=240–241 |url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v063n02/p0240-p0241.pdf|doi=10.2307/4080015|jstor=4080015 }} *''E. s. mindanensis'' (Linnaeus, 1766) – (includes ''E. s. paraguena'') ([[Masauji Hachisuka|Hachisuka]], 1934) from [[Palawan]], and ''E. s. corvina'' ([[Erwin Stresemann|Stresemann]], 1931) from [[Halmahera]], the Philippines (including Palawan and the [[Babuyan Islands]]), islands between [[Mindanao]] and Sulawesi, and [[North Maluku]], except the [[Sula Islands]] == Description == [[File:Asian Koel Female.jpg|thumb|left|A female of the nominate subspecies]] The Asian koel is a large and long-tailed cuckoo measuring {{convert|39|-|46|cm|in|abbr=on}} and weighing {{convert|190|-|327|g|oz|abbr=on}}.''CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses'' by John B. Dunning Jr. (ed.). CRC Press (1992), {{ISBN|978-0-8493-4258-5}}.[http://www.oiseaux-birds.com/card-asian-koel.html Asian Koel]. oiseaux-birds.com The male of the nominate race is glossy bluish-black, with a pale greenish grey bill, the iris is crimson, and it has grey legs and feet. The female of the nominate race is brownish on the crown and has rufous streaks on the head. The back, rump and wing coverts are dark brown with white and buff spots. The underparts are whitish, but is heavily striped. The other subspecies differ in colouration and size.{{cite book|author=Payne, RB|year=2005|title=The Cuckoos|publisher=Oxford University Press}} The upper plumage of young birds is more like that of the male and they have a black beak.{{cite book|author1=Ali S |author2=Ripley, SD |name-list-style=amp|year=1981|pages=227–230|title=Handbook of the birds of India and Pakistan. Volume 3|publisher=Oxford University Press|edition=2nd}} They are very vocal during the breeding season (March to August in the Indian Subcontinent), with a range of different calls. The familiar song of the male is a repeated ''koo-Ooo''. The female makes a shrill ''kik-kik-kik...'' call. Calls vary across populations. They show a pattern of [[moult]] that differs from those of other parasitic cuckoos. The outer [[Flight feather#Primaries|primaries]] show a transilient (alternating) ascending moult (P9-7-5-10-8-6) while the inner primaries are moulted in stepwise descending order (1-2-3-4).(Payne citing Stresemann and Stresemann 1961) == Distribution and habitat == [[File:Asian Koel (Eudynamys scolopacea)- Male close up in Kolkata I IMG 7568.jpg|thumb|left|Adult male of nominate race (West Bengal, India) showing the crimson iris. Young birds have dark irides.{{cite journal|author=Daniels, RJR|year=1984|title=Eye color in koels|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=24|issue=9–10|page=13|url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW24#page/n74/mode/1up }}]] The Asian koel is a bird of light woodland and cultivation. It is a mainly resident breeder in tropical southern Asia from Iran, Pakistan, India, Bangladesh, Maldives, and Sri Lanka to southern China and the Greater Sundas. They have great potential in colonizing new areas, and were among the pioneer birds to colonize the volcanic island of [[Krakatau]].{{cite journal|journal= GeoJournal | title= Colonization of the Krakatau islands by vertebrates| volume= 28| issue=2 | pages= 225–231| year=1992|author1=Rawlinson PA |author2=RA Zann |author3=S Balen |author4=IWB Thornton |name-list-style=amp| doi= 10.1007/BF00177236| s2cid= 189887589}} They first arrived in Singapore in the 1980s and became very common birds. Some populations may make long-distance movements being found in places like Australia. == Behaviour == [[File:Asian Koel- Immature asking for food from House Crow I IMG 5786.jpg|thumb|upright|right|Immature female (nominate race) begging for food.]] The Asian koel is a [[brood parasite]], and lays its single [[Egg (biology)|egg]] in the nests of a variety of birds, including the [[jungle crow]],{{cite book|author=Goodwin D.|year=1983|title=Crows of the World |publisher=Queensland University Press, St Lucia, Qld|isbn=0-7022-1015-3}} and [[house crow]]. In Sri Lanka before 1880 it was only known to parasitize the jungle crow, later shifting to the house crow.{{cite journal|author=Phillips, WWA|year=1948|title= Cuckoo problems of Ceylon|journal=Spolia Zeylanica|volume=25|pages=45–60}} A study in India found 5% of ''[[Corvus splendens]]'' and 0.5% of ''[[Corvus macrorhynchos]]'' nests parasitized.{{cite journal|author=Lamba, BS|year=1976|title= The Indian crows: a contribution to their breeding biology, with notes on brood parasitism on them by the Indian Koel|journal=Records of the Zoological Survey of India|volume=71|issue=1–4 |pages=183–300|doi=10.26515/rzsi/v71/i1-4/1975/161963 |s2cid=251705301 |doi-access=free}} [[File:Asian Koel Being Fed by Black-collared Starling (cropped).jpg|thumb|An Asian koel being fed by a [[black-collared starling]].]] [[File:Eudynamys scolopaceus MHNT.ZOO.2010.11.152.12.jpg|thumb|''Eudynamys scolopaceus'' in a clutch of ''Corvus splendens'' - [[MHNT]]]] In Bangladesh, they parasitise [[long-tailed shrike]] (''Lanius schach''), [[common myna]] (''Acridotheres tristis'') and house crows (''Corvus splendens'') at about 35.7, 31.2 and 10.8% rates respectively.{{cite journal |title=Interactions between the Asian koel (Eudynamys scolopacea) and its hosts|author1=Begum, Sajeda |author2=Moksnes, Arne |author3=Røskaft, Eivin |author4=Stokke, Bård G. |journal= Behaviour| volume= 148| issue= 3| year=2011 | pages=325–340|doi=10.1163/000579511X558400}} Host nests at low heights and nearer to fruit trees tended to be preferred by koels.{{cite journal| journal= Journal of Ornithology| volume=152| issue= 3| year=2011| pages=793–800| doi=10.1007/s10336-011-0652-y| title=Factors influencing host nest use by the brood parasitic Asian Koel (''Eudynamys scolopacea'')|vauthors=Begum S, Moksnes A, Røskaft E, Stokke BG| s2cid=39665560}} In [[Southern Thailand]] and the [[Malay Peninsula]], koels have shifted host from crows to mynas (''[[Acridotheres]]'' sp.) as the latter became more common in the late 1900s. A record exists of [[black drongo]] raising a koel,{{cite journal|author=Smith, TEH|year=1950 |volume=49|issue=1|page=304 |title=Black drongos fostering a koel|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48181746}} and other hosts recorded include the [[European magpie]]{{cite journal|author=Harington, HH |year=1904 |title= The Koels laying in the nest of ''Pica rustica'', the Magpie |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society|volume=15|issue=3|page=520|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/2097152}} and possibly the [[black-headed oriole]].{{cite journal|vauthors=Sethi VK, Saxena V, Bhatt D |year=2006 |title=An instance of the Asian Koel ''Eudynamys scolopacea'' destroying the nest of a Black-headed Oriole ''Oriolus xanthornus''|journal= Indian Birds|volume=2|issue=6|pages=173–174}}{{cite web|author=Lowther, Peter E |url=http://fieldmuseum.org/sites/default/files/OWcuckoo-15jan2012.pdf |year=2007 |access-date=2009-06-09 |title=Host list of avian brood parasites −2 – Cuculiformes; Cuculidae |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121023210922/http://fieldmuseum.org/sites/default/files/OWcuckoo-15jan2012.pdf |archive-date=2012-10-23 |url-status=live}} In the Philippines, Asian Koels have been found to parasitize the cavity nest of Coletos (''Sarcops calvus'').{{Cite journal | author1-last= Gicaraya | author1-first=VAG | author2-last=Española | author2-first=CP | year=2023| title= Brood Parasitism of the Asian Koel Eudynamys scolopaceus on the Tree Cavity-Nesting Coleto Sarcops calvus: First Record in the Philippines |journal=Ornithological Science |volume=22 |issue=2 | pages=175–178 | doi=10.2326/osj.22.175| s2cid=260203491 | url=https://bioone.org/journals/ornithological-science/volume-22/issue-2/osj.22.175/Brood-Parasitism-of-the-Asian-Koel-Eudynamys-scolopaceus-on-the/10.2326/osj.22.175.short| url-access=subscription | doi-access=free }} Males may distract the hosts so that the female gets a chance to lay an egg in the nest.{{cite journal|author=Dewar, D|author-link=Douglas Dewar|year=1907 |title=An enquiry into the parasitic habits of the Indian koel|journal= Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society|volume=17|issue=3|pages=765–782|url= https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30119991}}{{cite journal|url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW8#page/n83/mode/1up |author=Raju, K.S.R. Krishna|year=1968| title=Intelligence of a pair of Koels ''Eudynamys scolopacea''| journal=Newsletter for Birdwatchers|volume=8|issue=10|page=12}} More often however, the female visits the nest of the host alone. The koel is not known to lay eggs in an empty host nest and a study in Pakistan found that the first koel eggs were laid, on average, within one and half days of the laying of the host's first egg.{{cite journal|author=Lamba, BS|year=1966 |title=The egg-laying of the Koel ''Eudynamys scolopacea'' (Linnaeus)|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society|volume=63|issue=3|pages=750–751|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47951143}} The chicks of the koel hatched about 3 days ahead of the host chicks.{{cite journal|author=Ali, H|author2=SA Hassan|author3=SA Rana|author4= MA Beg|author5=M Mehmood-ul-Hassan|name-list-style=amp|year=2007 |title=Brood parasitism of Asian koel (''Eudynamys scolopacea'') on the house crow (''Corvus splendens'') in Pothwar region of Pakistan|journal=Pakistan Journal of Agricultural Sciences |volume=44|issue=4|pages=627–634}} Koels usually lay only an egg or two in a single nest but as many as seven to eleven eggs have been reported from some host nests.{{cite journal|author=Jacob, JR|year=1915 |title=Seven Koel's eggs in one nest|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society|volume= 24|issue=1|pages=191–192|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30151728}}{{cite journal|author=Jones, AE|year=1916 |title=Number of Koel's ''E. honorata'' eggs found in one nest|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society|volume=24|issue=2|page=370|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30151935}}{{cite journal|author=Abdulali, H|year=1931 |title=Eleven Koel eggs in a Crow's nest|journal= Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society|volume=35|issue=2|page=458|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47540482}} A female may remove a host egg before laying. Eggs hatch in 12 to 14 days. The young koel does not always push out eggs or evict the host chicks, and initially calls like a crow. The young fledge in 20 to 28 days. Unlike some other cuckoos, the young do not attempt to kill the host chicks, a trait that is shared with the [[channel-billed cuckoo]]s which are also largely frugivorous as adults.{{cite journal|author=Broom, M|author2=G D Ruxton|author3=Rebecca M Kilner|name-list-style=amp|year=2007 |title=Host life-history strategies and the evolution of chick-killing by brood parasitic offspring|journal=Behavioral Ecology|doi=10.1093/beheco/arm096|volume=19|pages=22–34|doi-access=free|url=https://zenodo.org/records/896838/files/article.pdf}} Some scholars, [[Rebecca Kilner]] in particular, have suggested that koels, like some other brood parasites, do not evict the host chicks as a result of higher costs which might outweigh the benefits of evicting nestmates. A small parasite may not be able to evict large host eggs or chicks from a deep Corvid nest without risking starvation and possibly accidental self-eviction. An alternative hypothesis that retaining host chicks might benefit the koel chicks did not gain much support.{{cite journal|author=Grim, T|year=2006|title=Low virulence of brood parasitic chicks: adaptation or constraint|journal=Ornithological Science|volume=5|issue=2|pages=237–242|url=http://www.zoologie.upol.cz/osoby/Grim/Grim_Ornithol_Sci_2006.pdf|doi=10.2326/osj.5.237|access-date=2009-06-08|archive-date=2016-03-03|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303165741/http://www.zoologie.upol.cz/osoby/Grim/Grim_Ornithol_Sci_2006.pdf}} Adult female parents have been known to feed young koels in the nests of the hosts,{{cite book|author=Lydekker, R|author-link=Richard Lydekker|year=1895 |title=The Royal Natural History. Volume 4.|page=8}}{{cite journal|last=Dixit|first=Dhruv|year=1968 |title=Parental instincts in Koel ''Eudynamys scolopacea'' (Linnaeus)|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society|volume=65|issue=2|pages=485–486|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48068008}} a behaviour seen in some other brood parasitic species as well. Adult males have however not been noted to feed fledglings.{{cite journal|author1=Lorenzana, JC |author2=SG Sealy |name-list-style=amp|year=1998 |title=Adult brood parasites feeding nestlings and fledglings of their own species: A review|journal=Journal of Field Ornithology |volume=69|issue=3|pages=364–375|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jfo/v069n03/p0364-p0375.pdf}}{{cite journal|author=Fulton, R.|year=1904 |title=The Kohoperoa or Koekoea, Long-tailed Cuckoo (''Urodynamis taitensis''): an account of its habits, description of a nest containing its (supposed) egg, and a suggestion as to how the parasitic habit in birds has become established|journal=Transactions and Proceedings of the New Zealand Institute |volume=36|pages=113–148|url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/periodicals/TPRSNZ1903-36.2.5.1.8}} The Asian koel is omnivorous, consuming a variety of insects, caterpillars, eggs and small [[vertebrate]]s. Adults feed mainly on [[fruit]]. They will sometimes defend fruiting trees that they forage in and chase away other frugivores.{{cite journal|author=Pratt, Thane K.|date=1 May 1984 |title=Examples of tropical frugivores defending fruit-bearing plants|journal=The Condor|volume=86|pages=123–129|url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v086n02/p0123-p0129.pdf|doi=10.2307/1367024|issn=0010-5422|issue=2|first=T. K.|jstor=1367024}} They have been noted to be especially important in the [[seed dispersal|dispersal]] of the sandalwood tree (''[[Santalum album]]'') in India. Large seeded fruits are sometimes quickly regurgitated near the parent tree while small seeded fruits are ingested and are likely to be deposited at greater distances from the parent tree.{{cite journal|author=Hegde, S G|author2=R Uma Shaanker|author3=KN Ganeshaiah|name-list-style=amp|year=1991 |title=Evolution of seed size in the bird-dispersed tree ''Santalum album'' L.: a trade off between seedling establishment and dispersal efficiency|journal= Evol. Trends. Plants|volume= 5|pages=131–135}} They have a large gape and are capable of swallowing large fruits including the hard fruit of palms such as ''[[Arenga]]'' and ''[[Livistona]]''. They have been known to occasionally take eggs of small birds.{{cite journal|author=Uttangi, JC|year=2004 |title=Robbing of eggs by female Koel, from the nest of Red-whiskered Bulbul (''Pycnonotus jocosus'')|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=44|issue=5|page=77}}{{cite journal|author=Purefoy, A.E. Bagwell|year=1947 |title=The koel as an egg-stealer|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society|volume=46|issue=4|page=723|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48200376}} They feed on the fruits of ''[[Cascabela thevetia]]'' which are known to be toxic to mammals.{{cite journal|author=Kannan, R|year=1991 |title=Koels feeding on the yellow oleander|journal=Blackbuck|volume=7|issue=2|page=48}}{{cite journal|author=Krishnan, M|author-link=Madhaviah Krishnan|year=1952 |title= Koels (''Eudynamis scolopaceus'') eating the poisonous fruit of the Yellow Oleander|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=50|issue=4|pages=943–945|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48057630}} A number of parasites of the species have been described, including malaria-like protozoa, lice and nematodes.{{cite journal|doi= 10.2307/3274690|title= Observations on ''Plasmodium circumflexum'' Kikuth and ''P. vaughani'' Novy and Macneal from East Pakistan|jstor= 3274690|author1=Laird, Marshall |author2=Faiyaz A. Lari |name-list-style=amp|journal= The Journal of Parasitology |volume= 44 | issue= 2 |year=1958 |pages=136–152|pmid= 13539706 |doi-access= free}}{{cite journal|author1=Peirce, M. A. |author2=R. D. Adlard |name-list-style=amp| year= 2005| title= The haemoproteids of the Cuculidae| journal=Journal of Natural History|volume=39|issue=25| pages=2281–2287 |doi= 10.1080/00222930500060942 |bibcode=2005JNatH..39.2281P |s2cid=86325960 |url=http://publication.plazi.org/id/B52AA94B5F27FF9DFFF5FF85FFD6FF8F }}{{cite journal|title=On a new nematode ''Spirocaudata bispiculatum'' gen.nov. sp.nov. (Ancyracanthidae) from the koel, Eudynamys scolopacea| author=Sharma RK|journal=Parasitology| year=1971 |volume=62 |pages=49–52| doi= 10.1017/S0031182000071286| s2cid=86835425}} [[File:Asiatic Koel Female.jpg|none|thumb|213x213px|''Eudynamys scolopaceus scolopaceus, India'']] [[File:Asian koel at Chandigarh.jpg|thumb|Asian koel at Chandigarh.]] == In culture == The word "koel" is [[Onomatopoeia|onomatopoeic]] in origin. The Sanskrit name of "Kokila" and words in several Indian languages are similarly echoic.{{cite book|author=Yule, Henry|year=1903|url=https://dsal.uchicago.edu/cgi-bin/philologic/getobject.pl?c.1:1:344.hobson|title=Hobson-Jobson: A glossary of colloquial Anglo-Indian words and phrases, and of kindred terms, etymological, historical, geographical and discursive.|publisher=John Murray, London.|page=490|access-date=2009-06-09|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121211161737/http://dsal.uchicago.edu/cgi-bin/philologic/getobject.pl?c.1:1:344.hobson|archive-date=2012-12-11}} Being familiar birds with loud calls, references to them are common in folklore, myth and poetry.{{cite book|author=Archer, William George|year=1972|title= The blue grove: the poetry of the Uraons.|publisher= Ayer Publishing|isbn=0-8369-6920-0}}{{cite journal|author=Fuchs, Stephen|author-link=Stephen Fuchs|year=2000|title=Thirty Korku Dancing Songs|journal= Asian Folklore Studies|volume=59|issue=1|pages=109–140|doi=10.2307/1179030|jstor=1179030 }} It is traditionally held in high regard for its song{{cite book|author=Beveridge, Annette|title=The Babur-nama in English|year=1922|publisher=Luzac, London|url=https://archive.org/details/baburnamainengli02babuuoft|isbn=0-7189-0139-8}} and revered in the [[Manusmriti]], with a decree protecting them from harm.{{cite journal|title=Environmental Laws of Manu: A Concise Review|author1=Padhy S|author2=SK Dash |author3=R Mohapatra |name-list-style=amp|journal=J. Hum. Ecol.|volume=19|issue=1|pages=1–12|year=2006|doi=10.1080/09709274.2006.11905850 |s2cid=109985448 }} The [[Vedas]], Sanskrit literature dated to about 2000 BC referred to it as ''Anya-Vapa'' which has been translated as "that which was raised by others" (or "sown for others to reap"{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/stream/vedicindexofname01macduoft#page/24/mode/1up/ |page =24|title=Vedic index of names and subjects. Volume 1. |author1=Macdonell, AA |author2=A B Keith |name-list-style=amp| publisher=John Murray|place= London|year=1912}}).{{Cite book|title=Birds in Sanskrit literature|last=Dave|first=K.N.|publisher=Motilal Banarsidass|year=2005|isbn=81-208-1842-3|edition=Revised|pages=128}} This has been interpreted as the earliest written reference to brood parasitism.{{cite journal|author=Friedmann, Herbert|title=The History of our Knowledge of Avian Brood Parasitism |journal=Centaurus|volume=10|issue=4|pages=282–304|year=1965|doi=10.1111/j.1600-0498.1965.tb00628.x|pmid=5336649|bibcode=1965Cent...10..282F }} It has been chosen as the state bird by the Indian union territory of [[Puducherry (union territory)|Puducherry]].{{cite news|title=Puducherry comes out with list of State symbols|author=Ramakrishnan, Deepa|date=April 21, 2007|url=http://www.hindu.com/2007/04/21/stories/2007042103560200.htm|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071031012044/http://www.hindu.com/2007/04/21/stories/2007042103560200.htm|newspaper=[[The Hindu]]|archive-date=October 31, 2007}}{{cite journal|title=Vernacular Names of the Birds of the Indian Subcontinent|journal=Buceros|author=Anonymous|year=1998|volume=3|issue=1|url=https://archive.org/details/IndianBirdNames|format=PDF|pages=[https://archive.org/details/IndianBirdNames/page/n0 53]–109}} These birds were once very popular in India as cagebirds. Feeding even on boiled rice, these hardy birds lived in captivity for as long as 14 years.{{cite book|author=Law, Satya Churn|year=1923|title=Pet birds of Bengal. Vol. 1|publisher=Thacker and Spink|pages=[https://archive.org/details/petbirdsofbengal00laws/page/315 315]–316|url=https://archive.org/details/petbirdsofbengal00laws }} In Sri Lanka, Koel has an unbreakable bond with the [[Sinhalese New Year]] as according to the beliefs, the call of koels signify the arrival of the new year in the month of April. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further reading == * Eates, KR (1938) The status of the Koel (''Eudynamys scolopaceus'' L.) in Sind. ''Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society'' 40(2):328. * Jose, TV (1980) Koels. ''[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]]'' . 20(4), 17. * Menon, G.K.; Shah, R.V. (1979) Adaptive features in juvenal plumage pattern of the Indian Koel ''Eudynamys scolopacea'': host mimesis and hawk-pattern. ''Journal of the Yamashina Institute for Ornithology'' 11(2):87–95 * Neelakantan, KK (1980) The breeding of the Indian Koel ''Eudynamys scolopacea''. ''[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]]'' . 20(1):7. * Parasharya, BM (1985) Comments on eye color in the Koel ''Eudynamys scolopacea''. ''[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]]'' . 25(1–2), 11–12. * Ray-Chaudhuri, R. (1967) Mitotic and meiotic chromosomes of the koel ''Eudynamys scolopacea scolopacea''. ''Nucleus'' 10: 179–189. (Study notes that the W chromosome is the 6th largest one and has the centromere in a subterminal position.) * Ryall C (2003) Mimicry of a crow chick by an Asian koel ''Eudynamys scolopacea'' as a defence against attack by house crows Corvus splendens. ''Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society'' 100(1):136–137 * Santharam, V (1979) Crows feeding young koel. ''[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]]'' . 19(7), 4. * Sarkar AK, Maitra SK, Midya T. (1976) Histological, histochemical and biochemical effects of cadmium chloride in female koel (''Eudynamys scolopacea''). ''Acta Histochem''. 57(2):205-11. * Sarkar AK, Maitra SK, Midya T. (1977) Effects of reserpine on female reproductive organs of the Indian koel ''Eudynamys scolopacea scolopacea'' (L). ''Indian Journal of Experimental Biology'' 15(5):349-51. {{Commons category|Eudynamys scolopaceus}} {{Wikispecies|Eudynamys scolopaceus}} {{Brood parasite}} {{Taxonbar |from=Q798401}} [[Category:Brood parasites]] [[Category:Eudynamys|Asian koel]] [[Category:Birds of Asia]] [[Category:Birds of Indomalaya]] [[Category:Birds described in 1758|Asian koel]] [[Category:Animal taxa named by Carl Linnaeus|Asian koel]] [[Category:Symbols of Jharkhand]]

zh.extract:
噪鹃（学名：Eudynamys scolopaceus）为杜鹃科噪鹃属的鸟类，俗名嫂鸟、鬼郭公、哥好雀、婆好、叫春鳥、升Key雀、哦啊、噪鳩。 它分佈於南亞、東亞和東南亞。
它與密切相關的黑嘴噪鵑和太平洋噪鵑一起形成一個超種，這些物種有時被視為亞種。噪鵑像許多與其相關的杜鵑一樣，是一種巢寄生者，會將其卵產在烏鴉和其他寄主的巢中，由寄主養育其幼鳥。牠們在杜鵑中不尋常之處在於成年後主要以果食性為主。 "koel"這個名稱的起源具有模仿聲音的特點，並在多種語言中有不同的變體。這種鳥在印度和尼泊爾的詩歌中是廣泛使用的象徵。


分類
1747年，英國博物學家喬治·愛德華茲在他的《罕見鳥類的自然史》（Natural History of Uncommon Birds）第二卷中包括了一幅噪鵑的插圖和描述。他使用了英文名稱 "The Brown and Spotted Indian Cuckow"。愛德華茲的手工上色版畫是基於來自孟加拉的標本，該標本屬於倫敦絲綢圖案設計師兼博物學家約瑟夫·丹德里奇。 1758年，瑞典博物學家卡爾·林奈在更新他第十版的《自然系統》時，將噪鵑與其他杜鵑一起歸入杜鵑屬（Cuculus）。林奈簡要描述了這個物種，創造了雙名法Cuculus scolopaceus，並引用了愛德華茲的作品。 如今，噪鵑被歸入噪鵑屬（Eudynamys），該屬由英國博物學家尼古拉斯·艾爾沃德·維戈斯和托馬斯·霍斯菲爾德於1827年引入。 這個屬名Eudynamys由古希臘語eu（意為 "優良"）和dunamis（意為 "力量" 或 "強度"）組成。種小名scolopaceus 是現代拉丁語，意為 "類似鷸" ，源自拉丁語scolopax，意為 "鷸" 或 "丘鷸"。
該物種在其廣泛的分佈範圍內存在變異，擁有多個島嶼種群，並且提出了多種分類變異的建議。黑嘴噪鵑 （E. melanorhynchus） 在蘇拉威西地區以及太平洋噪鵑在澳大拉西亞有時被認為與噪鵑是同種，在這種情況下，這個“合併”物種被稱為普通噪鵑（common koel）。由於在羽毛、喙的顏色和叫聲上的差異，這三者越來越多地被視為不同的物種。 另外，僅有黑嘴噪鵑曾被認為是單獨的物種，或者噪鵑包含了除在澳大利亞繁殖的亞種外所有通常歸入太平洋噪鵑的亞種，這樣該亞種則被稱為澳大利亞噪鵑 （E. cyanocephalus）。
噪鵑有幾個地理型態，這些型態具有明顯的羽毛差異或在地理上隔離，基因流動較少。以下是根據佩恩所列的命名亞種及其分佈和同物異名：

噪鹃指名亚种 E. s. scolopaceus （林奈, 1758） – 巴基斯坦、印度、尼泊爾、孟加拉國、斯里蘭卡、拉克沙群島和馬爾代夫
噪鹃华南亚种 E. s. chinensis 卡巴尼斯 和 海涅, 1863 – 印度支那（除泰馬來半島）、台湾岛以及中国大陆自四川以东向秦岭至淮河、长江口一帶及以南至海南等地。该物种的模式产地在广州。
噪鹃海南亚种 E. s. harterti 英格拉姆, C 1912 – 海南特有亞種。该物种的模式产地在海南海口。
E. s. malayana 卡巴尼斯 和 海涅, 1863 – 泰馬來半島、小巽他群島和大巽他群島，除蘇拉威西外。可能包括從安達曼群島和尼科巴群島 描述的dolosa 亞種
E. s. mindanensis （林奈, 1766） – （包括E. s. paraguena） （Hachisuka, 1934） 從巴拉望，以及E. s. corvina （Stresemann, 1931） 從哈馬黑拉島，菲律賓（包括巴拉望和巴布延群島），島嶼位於民答那峨和蘇拉威西之間，以及北馬魯古，除蘇拉群島外


描述
  
噪鵑是一種大型且尾巴較長的杜鵑，體長39—46 cm（15—18英寸），重190—327 g（6.7—11.5 oz）。指名亞種的雄鳥為光滑的藍黑色，喙呈淡綠灰色，虹膜為深紅色，腿和腳為灰色。指名亞種的雌鳥頭頂呈棕色，並在頭部有紅褐色的條紋。背部、尾部和翼上覆羽為深棕色，帶有白色和黃色斑點。下腹部呈白色，但有明顯的條紋。其他亞種在顏色和大小上有所不同。幼鳥的上部羽毛更像雄鳥，喙為黑色。  
在繁殖季節（在印度次大陸為3月至8月），牠們非常喧鬧，發出各種不同的叫聲，包括深夜。雄鳥熟悉的叫聲是重複的koo-Ooo。雌鳥發出尖銳的kik-kik-kik...叫聲。不同族群的叫聲也有所不同。 由於重複叫時的聲調會不斷提高，在香港它被稱為「升Key雀」。
牠們展現出與其他寄生杜鵑不同的換羽模式。外側的初級飛行羽顯示出一種跳躍式的上升換羽（P9-7-5-10-8-6），而內側的初級飛行羽則以逐步下降的順序換羽（1-2-3-4）（佩恩引述斯特雷塞曼和斯特雷塞曼1961年）。


分佈和棲地
  
噪鵑棲息於輕度森林和農耕地。牠們主要在南亞熱帶地區繁殖，分佈於伊朗、巴基斯坦、印度、孟加拉國、馬爾代夫和斯里蘭卡，直到中國南部和大巽他群島。牠們在拓殖新區方面表現出強大的潛力，並且是最早拓殖喀拉喀托火山火山島的鳥類之一。牠們於1980年代首次抵達新加坡，並成為非常常見的鳥類。
某些族群可能會進行長距離遷徙，出現在如澳大利亞等地。

分布于印度、斯里兰卡、南太平洋诸岛、台湾以及中国大陆的自四川东向秦岭至淮河、长江口及这范围以南大陆、海南等地。在伊朗，阿聯酋及也門有迷鳥記錄。噪鹃一般活动于居民点附近树木茂盛的地方，从山地的大森林至丘陵以及村边的疏林都有踪迹。该物种的模式产地在孟加拉国。


行為
  
噪鵑是一種巢寄生者，將其單枚卵產在各種鳥類的巢中，包括叢林鴉和家鴉。在斯里蘭卡，1880年前牠們僅寄生於叢林鴉，後來轉而寄生於家鴉。印度的一項研究發現，5%的家鴉（Corvus splendens）和0.5%的巨嘴鴉（Corvus macrorhynchos）巢遭到寄生。  

  
在孟加拉國，牠們以約35.7%、31.2%和10.8%的比例寄生於棕背伯勞 （Lanius schach）、家八哥 （Acridotheres tristis） 和家鴉 （Corvus splendens） 的巢。位於低處且靠近果樹的巢更容易被噪鵑選擇。在泰國南部和馬來半島，隨著1900年代晚期家八哥的增多，噪鵑的寄主從烏鴉轉移到了家八哥 （Acridotheres sp.）。曾有紀錄顯示大卷尾養育過噪鵑，其他已記錄的寄主包括歐亞喜鵲，可能還有黑頭黃鸝。在菲律賓，噪鵑已被發現寄生於科萊托鳥 （Sarcops calvus） 的樹洞巢。雄鳥可能會分散寄主的注意力，以便雌鳥有機會在巢中產卵。然而，更常見的是，雌鳥單獨訪問寄主的巢。已知噪鵑不會在空巢中產卵，一項在巴基斯坦的研究發現，噪鵑第一枚卵的產下時間平均在寄主的第一枚卵產下後一天半內。噪鵑的雛鳥通常比寄主的雛鳥早約3天孵化。噪鵑通常在一個巢中只產一兩枚卵，但有些寄主巢中報告過多達七到十一枚卵的情況。雌鳥在產卵前可能會移走寄主的一枚卵。卵在12至14天內孵化。年輕的噪鵑並不總是會把蛋推出去或驅逐寄主的小鳥，並且起初叫聲像烏鴉。幼鳥在20到28天內離巢。與其他杜鵑不同，噪鵑幼鳥不會試圖殺死寄主的雛鳥，這一特徵與鉤嘴杜鵑相似，後者成年後也是以果食為主。一些學者，特別是Rebecca Kilner，認為噪鵑與一些其他巢寄生者一樣，不會逐出寄主的雛鳥，是因為逐出巢友的代價可能大於收益。一隻小型的寄生者可能無法將寄主的大卵或雛鳥從深巢中逐出，而不冒著餓死或意外逐出自己的風險。另一種假說認為，保留寄主的雛鳥可能對噪鵑雛鳥有利，但未獲得太多支持。已知雌性親鳥會在寄主巢中餵養噪鵑幼鳥，這種行為在一些其他巢寄生鳥類中也有出現。然而，雄性成鳥並未被觀察到餵養離巢的幼鳥。
噪鵑是雜食性動物，攝食各種昆蟲、毛蟲、卵和小型脊椎動物。成鳥主要以水果為食。牠們有時會保護自己取食的果樹，並驅逐其他食果鳥。已觀察到牠們在印度的檀香樹 （Santalum album） 的種子散播中扮演著特別重要的角色。大種子的水果有時會迅速在母樹附近被反芻，而小種子的水果則會被吞食，並可能在距離母樹更遠的地方散播。牠們的喉嚨大，能夠吞食大型水果，包括如山棕屬（Arenga）和蒲葵屬（Livistona）等棕櫚科植物的堅硬果實。已知牠們偶爾會取食小鳥的卵。
牠們還取食黃花夾竹桃（Cascabela thevetia）的果實，這種果實對哺乳動物有毒。
已描述了多種寄生於噪鵑的寄生蟲，包括類似瘧疾的原生動物、蝨子和線蟲。

  


文化中的影響
「koel」這個詞源自擬聲詞。梵語中的名字「Kokila」以及幾種印度語言中的詞彙也同樣具有回聲效果。由於這些鳥以熟悉的叫聲聞名，在民間傳說、神話和詩歌中對它們的引用十分常見。牠們的歌聲傳統上受到高度讚揚，並在《摩奴法典》中受到尊崇，法典中有保護牠們免受傷害的法令。《吠陀》和梵文文學可追溯至約公元前2000年，稱其為Anya-Vapa，這個詞被翻譯為「由他人撫養的」或「為他人播種以供收穫」。這被解釋為最早記錄的巢寄生行為的參考。牠們被選為印度聯邦屬地本地治里的州鳥。
這些鳥類曾在印度作為籠中鳥非常受歡迎。牠們甚至可以進食煮熟的米飯，這些耐寒的鳥類在圈養環境下可存活長達14年。
